vocabulary.definition: making you feel relaxed and free from pain

vocabulary.definition: a thick soft cover filled with feathers that you sleep under

vocabulary.definition: an electric oven that cooks or heats food very quickly

vocabulary.definition: almost never

vocabulary.definition: a piece of furniture with drawers for keeping clothes

vocabulary.definition: easy to use or situated nearby
vocabulary.example: Living close to school is very convenient.

vocabulary.definition: relating to the home and the people who live in it
vocabulary.example: We share household chores equally.

vocabulary.definition: the usual order in which you do things

vocabulary.definition: a house that is part of a row of houses joined together
vocabulary.example: They live in a terraced house in the city centre.

vocabulary.definition: having a lot of space; large in area
vocabulary.example: The living room is very spacious.

vocabulary.definition: a platform attached to the outside of a building above ground level

grammar.explanation: Use Present Simple for habits, routines, and permanent states. Use Present Continuous for actions happening now or temporary situations.
rules: Present Simple: I/You/We/They + base verb; He/She/It + verb+s
Present Continuous: am/is/are + verb+ing
State verbs (know, love, believe) DON'T use continuous

exercises.sentence: She _____ (usually/make) her bed every morning.
Be quiet! The baby _____ (sleep) right now.
I _____ (not/understand) why she is upset.
exercises.explanation: 'Understand' is a state verb — no continuous form.

exercises.sentence: My parents _____ (renovate) our kitchen this week.
Water _____ (boil) at 100 degrees Celsius.
She _____ (always/leave) her keys on the table.
exercises.explanation: Habit → Present Simple (though 'is always leaving' is also possible to show annoyance).

reading.title: My Unusual Home
reading.text:
My name is Sonia and I live in a very unusual place — a houseboat on the River Thames in London. Most people are surprised when I tell them this, because they imagine living on a boat must be very uncomfortable. In fact, my home is quite spacious inside: it has two bedrooms, a kitchen, a bathroom and a living room with large windows that look out onto the river.

The best thing about living on the water is waking up and seeing the river every morning. I love watching the boats go past while I have my breakfast. The neighbourhood is also very friendly. I know all my neighbours well, and we occasionally have barbecues on the riverside together.

Of course, there are some disadvantages. The boat moves a little in rough weather, which some guests find frightening. Parking is also a problem — I have to walk quite far to get to my car. However, I wouldn't swap my houseboat for a house on land. It's my perfect home.

questions.question: How do most people react when Sonia tells them where she lives?
questions.explanation: The text says 'Most people are surprised when I tell them this.'

questions.question: What does Sonia enjoy doing in the mornings?
options: C. Watching boats while having breakfast
questions.explanation: 'I love watching the boats go past while I have my breakfast.'

questions.question: What is one disadvantage of living on a houseboat?
options: B. It moves in bad weather
questions.explanation: 'The boat moves a little in rough weather, which some guests find frightening.'

questions.question: Overall, how does Sonia feel about her home?
options: A. She wishes she lived on land
B. She finds it too expensive
C. She is very happy with it
D. She wants a bigger home
questions.explanation: 'I wouldn't swap my houseboat for a house on land. It's my perfect home.'

sentence_transformations.original: It's a long time since I last visited my grandparents.
sentence_transformations.answer: I haven't visited my grandparents for a long time.

sentence_transformations.original: Living in the suburbs is cheaper than living in the city.
sentence_transformations.answer: Living in the city is more expensive than living in the suburbs.

sentence_transformations.original: My sister usually goes to bed at 10 pm.
sentence_transformations.answer: My sister usually goes to bed at 10 pm.
sentence_transformations.hint: No change needed — note the structure 'goes to bed'

sentence_transformations.original: They started building the new house three years ago.
sentence_transformations.answer: They have been building the new house for three years.

cloze.title: Urban vs Rural Living
cloze.text: Many young people today are choosing to (1)_____ in cities rather than in the countryside. There are many reasons for this. City life (2)_____ a wide range of job opportunities, and public transport makes it easy to (3)_____ around. However, life in a busy city can (4)_____ be stressful and expensive. Rent prices are much (5)_____ in cities than in rural areas. On the other hand, the countryside (6)_____ fresh air and a quieter lifestyle, which many people find (7)_____.

vocabulary.definition: required by law or rules; not optional
vocabulary.example: Education is compulsory for children aged 5–16.

vocabulary.definition: to give all your attention to something

vocabulary.definition: tools or machines needed for a particular purpose

vocabulary.definition: facts and information that you know

vocabulary.definition: training that makes people obey rules; a subject of study

vocabulary.definition: a list showing when events or activities are planned to happen

vocabulary.definition: not required; available for you to choose

grammar.explanation: Countable nouns have singular and plural forms and can be used with 'a/an'. Uncountable nouns have no plural form and cannot be used with 'a/an'.
rules: Countable: a book, two books, several students
Uncountable: information, advice, homework, furniture, knowledge, equipment
Some quantifiers work with both: some, any, a lot of, enough, no
Much/little = uncountable; Many/few = countable

exercises.sentence: The teacher gave us too much _____.
exercises.explanation: 'Homework' is uncountable — no plural, no article.

exercises.sentence: Can you give me some _____ about the course?
We don't have _____ time before the exam.
There are _____ students in my class.
The school has bought new _____ for the science lab.
exercises.explanation: 'Equipment' is uncountable. Use 'pieces of equipment' for quantity.

exercises.sentence: She gave me a very useful piece of _____.
exercises.explanation: 'Advice' is uncountable — use 'a piece of advice'.

reading.title: A School in the Cloud
reading.text:
In 1999, scientist Sugata Mitra conducted an unusual experiment. He placed a computer in a wall in a slum area of Delhi and left it for children to discover. He returned a few months later to find that the children had not only learned how to use the computer, but had also taught themselves basic English in order to use it more effectively.

This experiment led Mitra to develop his 'Hole in the Wall' project and, later, the concept of 'Self-Organised Learning Environments' (SOLEs). His idea is simple: give children access to the internet and a 'big question' to explore, and they will learn collaboratively without a teacher telling them what to do.

Mitra won the prestigious TED Prize in 2013 for his work and used the prize money to set up Schools in the Cloud around the world. These are not traditional classrooms — they are spaces where children can connect with each other and with mentors via video call. The results suggest that children are naturally curious and can motivate themselves to learn when given the right environment.

questions.question: What did Mitra discover when he returned to the experiment?
options: A. The computer had been stolen
B. The children had taught themselves to use it
C. The children were not interested in computers
D. Adults had been using the computer
questions.explanation: The text says children 'had learned how to use the computer'.

questions.question: What is the key principle of Mitra's SOLE approach?
options: A. Teachers guide students step by step
B. Students work alone with textbooks
C. Students learn collaboratively with internet access
D. Schools must have modern equipment
questions.explanation: Children 'learn collaboratively without a teacher telling them what to do'.

questions.question: What did Mitra use the TED Prize money for?
options: B. To fund more experiments in Delhi
C. To create Schools in the Cloud
questions.explanation: 'He used the prize money to set up Schools in the Cloud.'

questions.question: What does the article suggest about children?
options: A. They need strict discipline to learn
B. They learn best in traditional classrooms
C. They are naturally curious and self-motivated
D. They prefer to learn from computers only
questions.explanation: 'Children are naturally curious and can motivate themselves to learn.'

sentence_transformations.original: There are very few students who enjoy exams.
sentence_transformations.answer: Hardly any students enjoy exams.

sentence_transformations.original: It wasn't necessary for him to study all night.
sentence_transformations.answer: He didn't need to study all night.

sentence_transformations.original: The last time she studied French was three years ago.
sentence_transformations.answer: She hasn't studied French for three years.

sentence_transformations.original: Despite being tired, she finished her homework.
sentence_transformations.answer: Although she was tired, she finished her homework.

cloze.title: The Benefits of Reading
cloze.text: Reading is one of the most (1)_____ skills a student can develop. It not only helps you (2)_____ your vocabulary, but it also improves your ability to concentrate and think critically. Research (3)_____ that students who read regularly tend to (4)_____ better in all subjects, not just English. This is because reading (5)_____ you with knowledge about a wide range of topics. Teachers often recommend that students (6)_____ at least 30 minutes every day, even if it is just reading a magazine or website. The important thing is to make it a regular (7)_____.

grammar.explanation: Use Past Simple for completed actions at a specific time in the past. Use Present Perfect for actions with a connection to the present, or when no specific time is given.
rules: Past Simple: yesterday, last week, in 2020, when I was...
Present Perfect: ever, never, already, yet, just, recently, for, since
Present Perfect: result in the present is important
Have/has + past participle (gone, seen, done, played)

exercises.sentence: I _____ (never/try) surfing before.
She _____ (win) the gold medal at the 2022 championships.
_____ you ever _____ (play) chess?
We _____ (just/finish) our art project.
He _____ (start) his photography hobby when he _____ (be) twelve.
exercises.explanation: Specific time in past (when he was) → Past Simple both.

exercises.sentence: I _____ (not/see) that film yet. Is it good?

reading.title: The Rise of Esports
reading.text:
For many years, sport meant physical activity — running, swimming, or playing team games. However, in recent years, a new type of competition has emerged: esports, or competitive video gaming. Today, millions of people worldwide watch professional gamers compete in tournaments, and prize money can reach into the millions of dollars.

Esports fans argue that gaming requires just as much skill, strategy, and teamwork as traditional sports. Top players train for hours every day, developing fast reactions and detailed knowledge of complex game mechanics. Some universities in the USA now even offer esports scholarships to attract talented students.

Critics, however, point out that spending too much time in front of a screen can have negative health effects, including poor posture and eyesight problems. There are also concerns about the social isolation that excessive gaming can cause.

Despite these concerns, the esports industry continues to grow rapidly. Major brands are investing in teams and events, and the question is no longer whether esports is a real sport, but how large it will become.

questions.question: What does the article say has recently changed about sport?
options: A. Traditional sports are becoming less popular
B. A new form of competition has developed
C. Prize money for all sports has increased
D. Physical training has become more important
questions.explanation: 'A new type of competition has emerged: esports.'

questions.question: How do esports supporters justify calling it a sport?
options: A. It is watched by millions of people
B. Players earn a lot of money
C. It requires skill, strategy, and teamwork
questions.explanation: 'Gaming requires just as much skill, strategy, and teamwork as traditional sports.'

questions.question: What concern do critics raise?
options: B. Professional players earn too much
C. Too much screen time harms health
questions.explanation: 'Too much time in front of a screen can have negative health effects.'

questions.question: What does the article predict about esports?
options: A. It will replace traditional sports
B. It will continue to grow
C. It will lose popularity soon
D. It will become an Olympic sport
questions.explanation: 'The esports industry continues to grow rapidly.'

sentence_transformations.answer: She has been learning the guitar for two years.

Experts recommend choosing a hobby that (5)_____ you a sense of achievement. Whether it's painting, coding, or learning a musical (6)_____, the key is to find something you are (7)_____ about and commit to it regularly.

grammar.explanation: Comparatives compare two things; superlatives compare one thing to all others in a group.
rules: Short adj: cheaper, bigger, hotter; the cheapest, the biggest
Long adj: more comfortable, less expensive; the most comfortable
Irregular: good/better/best; bad/worse/worst; far/further/furthest
As...as: 'This hotel is as expensive as that one' (equal comparison)

exercises.sentence: Travelling by train is _____ than flying.
This is _____ beach I've ever visited.
The weather in Spain is _____ than in England.
This trip wasn't _____ our last one.
Which is _____ country in the world?
The _____ we booked, the _____ the prices were.

reading.title: Staycations: The New Holiday Trend
reading.text:
In recent years, a growing number of people have chosen to stay at home for their holidays rather than travelling abroad. This trend, known as a 'staycation', has become increasingly popular due to rising travel costs and environmental concerns.

Proponents of staycations argue that there are many advantages to exploring your own country. Firstly, it is significantly cheaper — you avoid expensive flights, airport taxes, and foreign currency costs. Secondly, you are likely to discover hidden gems close to home that you might never have noticed before. Many people are surprised by how much there is to see and do in their own region.

However, others feel that travelling abroad offers experiences that a staycation simply cannot match. Immersing yourself in a different culture, hearing a foreign language, and tasting unfamiliar food are all experiences that broaden your mind and perspective.

Regardless of which option you prefer, the most important thing is to take a break. Research consistently shows that holidays — whether at home or abroad — significantly improve mental health and productivity.

questions.question: Why have staycations become more popular?
options: A. People prefer their own culture
C. Travel costs and environmental worries have increased
D. Foreign holidays are no longer available
questions.explanation: 'Due to rising travel costs and environmental concerns.'

questions.question: What is one benefit of a staycation mentioned in the article?
options: A. You can speak a foreign language
B. You might discover places you didn't know about
C. The food is more varied
questions.explanation: 'You are likely to discover hidden gems close to home.'

questions.question: What do supporters of foreign travel say?
options: A. It is cheaper than staying at home
B. It helps you understand different cultures
C. There is no culture in your own country
questions.explanation: 'Immersing yourself in a different culture...broadens your mind.'

questions.question: What does research show about holidays in general?
options: B. Only foreign holidays are beneficial
C. They improve mental health and productivity
D. People prefer not to take them
questions.explanation: 'Holidays significantly improve mental health and productivity.'

sentence_transformations.original: No other hotel in the city is as luxurious as this one.
sentence_transformations.answer: This is the most luxurious hotel in the city.

sentence_transformations.original: The journey took longer than we expected.
sentence_transformations.answer: The journey wasn't as short as we expected.

sentence_transformations.original: She has never been to a hotter country.
sentence_transformations.answer: It is the hottest country she has ever been to.

sentence_transformations.original: Travelling by bus is cheaper, but it takes more time.
sentence_transformations.answer: Although travelling by bus is cheaper, it takes more time.

cloze.title: Packing Tips for Travellers
cloze.text: One of the most common (1)_____ travellers make is packing too much. When you are preparing for a holiday, it is tempting to (2)_____ everything you might possibly need. However, a heavy suitcase can (3)_____ your journey much more stressful. Experienced travellers (4)_____ to pack light, choosing versatile clothing items that can be (5)_____ in different ways. It is also a good idea to keep a list of (6)_____ items so you never forget anything important, such as your passport or travel insurance (7)_____.

vocabulary.definition: feeling sure about your abilities

vocabulary.definition: the way you feel at a particular time

vocabulary.definition: to deal with a difficult situation

vocabulary.definition: believing that good things will happen

vocabulary.definition: unhappy because someone else has something you want

vocabulary.definition: feeling uncomfortable because of something silly you did

vocabulary.definition: great sadness, especially after losing someone

vocabulary.definition: able to recover quickly from difficulties
vocabulary.example: Children are often more resilient than adults think.

vocabulary.definition: confidence in your own worth and abilities

vocabulary.definition: a state of ease; to make someone feel better
vocabulary.example: She called her friend for comfort.

grammar.topic: Modal Verbs (must, should, can, might, need to)
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs express necessity, obligation, possibility, ability, and advice. They are always followed by the base form of the verb.
rules: must / have to = obligation or necessity
should / ought to = advice or recommendation
can / could = ability or possibility
might / may = possibility (less certain)
mustn't = prohibition (not allowed); don't have to = not necessary

exercises.sentence: You _____ tell anyone about this — it's a secret.
I'm not sure, but she _____ be at the library.
You _____ bring your passport — it's the law.
You look tired. You _____ take a break.
She _____ speak three languages — she's very talented.
He _____ pass his driving test yesterday. He's so happy!
exercises.explanation: 'Managed to' or 'was able to' = succeeded at a specific past event. 'Could' is for general past ability.

reading.title: The Science of Happiness
reading.text:
Happiness is something most people wish they had more of, but what exactly is it? According to psychologists, happiness is not just about feeling good in the moment — it is about having a sense of purpose and meaning in your life.

Research consistently shows that people who maintain close relationships tend to be happier than those who are more isolated. Having friends and family to support you during difficult times significantly improves your ability to cope with stress. Interestingly, studies show that giving to others — whether through charity, time, or kind words — actually makes the giver feel happier too.

Another key factor in happiness is gratitude. People who regularly take time to appreciate what they have, rather than focusing on what they lack, report higher levels of life satisfaction. Keeping a gratitude journal, where you write down three things you are thankful for each day, is a simple technique that researchers have found to be genuinely effective.

Finally, physical activity plays an important role. Exercise releases chemicals in the brain that improve mood and reduce anxiety. Even a 20-minute walk can have a significant positive effect on how you feel.

questions.question: How do psychologists define happiness?
options: A. Feeling good all the time
B. Having purpose and meaning in life
questions.explanation: 'Happiness is not just about feeling good — it is about having a sense of purpose.'

questions.question: What does research show about relationships and happiness?
options: B. Close relationships help people cope better
C. Online friendships are most beneficial
questions.explanation: 'Having friends and family to support you significantly improves your ability to cope.'

questions.question: What is a gratitude journal used for?
questions.explanation: 'You write down three things you are thankful for each day.'

questions.question: Why is exercise mentioned as a factor in happiness?
options: A. It makes you look better
B. It helps you meet new people
C. It releases brain chemicals that improve mood
questions.explanation: 'Exercise releases chemicals in the brain that improve mood.'

sentence_transformations.original: It isn't necessary for you to come if you don't feel well.
sentence_transformations.answer: You don't have to come if you don't feel well.

sentence_transformations.original: My advice is to talk to someone you trust.
sentence_transformations.answer: You should talk to someone you trust.

sentence_transformations.original: I'm certain she is telling the truth.
sentence_transformations.answer: She must be telling the truth.

sentence_transformations.original: Perhaps the event was cancelled because of the weather.
sentence_transformations.answer: The event might have been cancelled because of the weather.

cloze.title: Managing Exam Stress
cloze.text: Many students (1)_____ anxious about exams, particularly when the pressure to (2)_____ well is very high. However, there are (3)_____ steps you can take to manage this stress. Firstly, make sure you start revising (4)_____ — leaving everything to the last minute only increases anxiety. Secondly, take regular breaks (5)_____ studying, as your brain needs rest to retain information. Getting enough sleep is (6)_____ important, as tiredness can seriously affect your (7)_____ to focus and think clearly.

vocabulary.example: The film was praised by critics but not audiences.

vocabulary.definition: the practice of officially examining material and removing unsuitable content
vocabulary.example: There is debate about censorship of online content.

grammar.topic: Relative Clauses (who, which, that, where, whose)
grammar.explanation: Relative clauses give more information about a noun. Defining relative clauses identify which person or thing you mean. Non-defining clauses add extra information (use commas).
rules: who = people; which = things; that = people or things (defining only)
where = places; whose = possession
Defining: no commas — 'The film that won the Oscar was brilliant'
Non-defining: commas — 'The director, who is French, won an award'

exercises.sentence: The actress _____ won the Oscar gave a brilliant speech.
This is the cinema _____ I saw my first film.
The novel, _____ was written in 1960, is still popular today.
I know a director _____ films have won many awards.
The song _____ played at the concert was beautiful.
She is the person _____ recommended this documentary to me.

reading.title: The Podcast Revolution
reading.text:
Over the past decade, podcasts have transformed the way millions of people consume media. A podcast is an audio programme that can be downloaded or streamed on demand, covering almost any topic imaginable — from true crime and history to comedy and science.

The appeal of podcasts lies in their flexibility. Unlike television, which requires you to sit and watch, podcasts can be listened to while commuting, exercising, or doing household chores. This makes them perfect for busy people who want to keep learning and entertained throughout their day.

Another reason for the podcasting boom is the low barrier to entry. Anyone with a microphone and an internet connection can start their own podcast. This has led to an explosion of diverse voices and perspectives that traditional media often fails to represent. Independent creators can build loyal audiences of thousands — or even millions — without the backing of a major broadcasting company.

However, the sheer volume of content available can be overwhelming. With millions of podcasts to choose from, finding quality content requires time and effort. Critics also point out that without editorial oversight, misinformation can spread easily in the podcasting world.

questions.question: What makes podcasts different from television?
options: A. They cover more interesting topics
B. They can be listened to while doing other things
C. They are produced by professionals only
D. They are available 24 hours a day
questions.explanation: 'Podcasts can be listened to while commuting, exercising, or doing household chores.'

questions.question: Why has podcasting grown so rapidly?
options: A. Large companies are investing heavily in it
B. Traditional media has become less popular
C. Anyone can easily start a podcast
D. Podcasts are free to listen to
questions.explanation: 'Anyone with a microphone and an internet connection can start their own podcast.'

questions.question: What problem do critics identify with podcasting?
options: B. Lack of variety in topics
questions.explanation: 'Without editorial oversight, misinformation can spread easily.'

questions.question: What is described as a disadvantage for listeners?
options: A. The content is too short
B. It costs too much to subscribe
C. There is too much content to choose from
D. Podcasts are hard to find online
questions.explanation: 'The sheer volume of content available can be overwhelming.'

sentence_transformations.original: The film was so boring that I fell asleep.
sentence_transformations.answer: The film wasn't interesting enough to keep me awake.

sentence_transformations.original: I last watched a film at the cinema three months ago.
sentence_transformations.answer: I haven't been to the cinema for three months.

sentence_transformations.original: The director made the actors rehearse every day.
sentence_transformations.answer: The actors were made to rehearse every day.

sentence_transformations.original: Despite the poor reviews, we enjoyed the show.
sentence_transformations.answer: We enjoyed the show even though it had poor reviews.

cloze.title: Music Streaming
cloze.text: The music industry has changed (1)_____ in the past 20 years. Before streaming services existed, people (2)_____ buy CDs or download songs individually. Now, for a monthly (3)_____, listeners can access millions of songs instantly. This has been great for consumers, but many artists (4)_____ that streaming pays them far less than CD sales once did. A musician needs millions of (5)_____ to earn the same amount they would have earned selling a few thousand albums. Despite this (6)_____, streaming services continue to (7)_____ in popularity, with hundreds of millions of subscribers worldwide.

vocabulary.definition: to travel regularly between home and work or school
vocabulary.example: She commutes by train every day.

vocabulary.definition: too much traffic causing slow movement

vocabulary.definition: the basic systems that support a society (roads, water, etc.)

vocabulary.definition: gas or other substance released into the air
vocabulary.example: Electric cars produce zero direct emissions.

vocabulary.definition: a person who is walking on a street
vocabulary.example: There are dedicated lanes for pedestrians in the city.

vocabulary.definition: the money you pay to travel on a bus, train, etc.
vocabulary.example: The bus fare has increased this year.

vocabulary.definition: to find your way, especially using a map or technology
vocabulary.example: I use my phone to navigate in unfamiliar cities.

vocabulary.definition: arriving or leaving at the expected time

vocabulary.definition: a road built to avoid a town centre

vocabulary.definition: a residential area on the edge of a city

grammar.explanation: The passive voice is used when the action is more important than who does it, or when we don't know who did it. Form: be + past participle.
rules: Active: Someone builds the road. Passive: The road is being built.
All tenses: is done, was done, has been done, will be done, is being done
By + agent (optional): The road was built by engineers.
Use passive in formal writing, science, and news

exercises.sentence: The new metro line _____ (build) next year.
exercises.explanation: Future passive: will + be + past participle.

exercises.sentence: The accident _____ (cause) by a reckless driver.
Traffic lights _____ (install) in cities to reduce accidents.
exercises.explanation: Present passive (general fact): are + past participle.

exercises.sentence: Passengers _____ (inform) about the delay through an announcement.
The bridge _____ (repair) at the moment, so expect delays.
exercises.explanation: Happening now → present continuous passive: is being + past participle.

exercises.sentence: Several new cycling routes _____ (create) in the city recently.
exercises.explanation: 'Recently' → present perfect passive: have been + past participle.

reading.title: The Electric Revolution in Transport
reading.text:
The global push to combat climate change has accelerated the development of electric vehicles (EVs). Governments around the world are setting ambitious targets — some countries have announced plans to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2035. In response, car manufacturers are investing billions into EV technology.

For consumers, the main attraction of electric vehicles is lower running costs. Electricity is significantly cheaper than petrol, and EVs require less maintenance because they have fewer moving parts. However, the higher upfront cost of purchasing an EV remains a barrier for many buyers.

One of the biggest challenges facing the widespread adoption of electric vehicles is charging infrastructure. Unlike petrol stations, which are found everywhere, public charging points are still relatively rare in many areas. Long-distance travellers often worry about running out of charge — a concern known as 'range anxiety'.

Despite these challenges, sales of electric vehicles have risen sharply in recent years. Analysts predict that by 2030, EVs could account for more than half of all new car sales globally. As battery technology improves and prices fall, the electric future seems increasingly inevitable.

questions.question: Why are governments promoting electric vehicles?
options: A. They are cheaper to produce
questions.explanation: 'The global push to combat climate change has accelerated the development of EVs.'

questions.question: What is 'range anxiety'?
options: B. Worry about the cost of charging
C. Concern about running out of battery charge
D. Anxiety about driving in cities
questions.explanation: 'Worry about running out of charge — a concern known as range anxiety.'

questions.question: What makes EVs cheaper to run?
options: A. They are built by cheaper manufacturers
C. Electricity is cheaper than petrol
questions.explanation: 'Electricity is significantly cheaper than petrol.'

questions.question: What does the article predict for 2030?
options: A. All petrol cars will be banned
B. EVs may make up half of new car sales
C. Charging infrastructure will be complete
questions.explanation: 'EVs could account for more than half of all new car sales globally.'

sentence_transformations.answer: A new road is being built outside the city.

cloze.title: Cycling in the City
cloze.text: Many cities are now (1)_____ cycling as a greener alternative to driving. Dedicated cycle lanes have been (2)_____ on major roads to make it safer for cyclists. In some cities, bike-sharing (3)_____ allow people to hire a bicycle for short trips. This reduces the (4)_____ of private cars on the road, which helps to lower pollution levels. However, cyclists still face challenges such as (5)_____ drivers and poorly maintained roads. Safety (6)_____, such as helmets and high-visibility jackets, are strongly recommended. Overall, cycling is not only good for the (7)_____, but also for your physical health.

vocabulary.definition: the power to affect someone's decisions or behaviour

grammar.explanation: Reported speech is used to tell someone what another person said. When reporting, verb tenses usually shift back one step (backshift). Pronouns and time expressions also change.
rules: Direct: 'I am tired.' → Reported: She said she was tired.
Present Simple → Past Simple; Present Continuous → Past Continuous
Past Simple → Past Perfect; will → would
Time changes: today → that day; yesterday → the day before; here → there

exercises.sentence: He said, 'I will post a video tomorrow.' → He said he _____ post a video _____.
She said, 'I have over a million followers.' → She said she _____ over a million followers.
exercises.explanation: Present Perfect → Past Perfect: 'had had' or Past Simple 'had' (both acceptable).

exercises.sentence: They told us, 'We are filming a new series.' → They told us they _____ a new series.
The journalist asked him where _____ (live).
exercises.explanation: Indirect questions use statement word order (not question order).

exercises.sentence: She said, 'Don't believe everything you read online.' → She told us not _____ everything we read online.
exercises.explanation: Reported imperatives: told + object + (not) to + infinitive.

exercises.sentence: He said he _____ (work) on a new project at the time.
exercises.explanation: Past Continuous in direct speech → Past Perfect Continuous in reported.

reading.title: The Reality Behind the Perfect Life
reading.text:
Scroll through any social media feed today and you will see endless images of people enjoying perfect lives — beautiful homes, exotic holidays, and flawless skin. But how much of what we see online is actually real?

Research suggests that the idealised images posted by influencers can have a negative impact on viewers' self-esteem, particularly among teenagers. When young people constantly compare themselves to carefully edited images, they may begin to feel that their own lives don't measure up.

However, a counter-movement is growing. A number of high-profile influencers have begun sharing unedited photos and discussing mental health struggles openly. This approach, often described as 'authentic content', has resonated strongly with audiences who are tired of feeling inadequate.

Platforms such as Instagram and TikTok have also introduced new regulations requiring influencers to disclose when a post is a paid advertisement. This is a step towards greater transparency, but critics argue that more needs to be done to protect young people from misleading content.

Ultimately, developing critical thinking skills — the ability to question and evaluate what we see online — may be the most powerful tool we have in navigating the digital world.

questions.question: What does research suggest about idealised social media images?
options: A. They motivate young people to work harder
B. They have no impact on how people feel
C. They can lower teenagers' self-esteem
D. They make social media more entertaining
questions.explanation: 'Idealised images can have a negative impact on viewers' self-esteem, particularly among teenagers.'

questions.question: What is 'authentic content' in this context?
options: B. Content featuring real, unedited images and honest topics
C. Content that is free of advertisements
D. Content only made by amateur creators
questions.explanation: 'Sharing unedited photos and discussing mental health struggles openly.'

questions.question: What new rule have platforms introduced?
options: C. Requiring influencers to label paid posts
questions.explanation: 'Requiring influencers to disclose when a post is a paid advertisement.'

questions.question: What does the article suggest is the best tool for young people?
options: D. Using platforms with strict regulations
questions.explanation: 'Developing critical thinking skills may be the most powerful tool.'

sentence_transformations.original: 'I don't know how many followers I have,' he said.

cloze.title: Building an Online Following
cloze.text: Growing a social media (1)_____ requires consistency and creativity. Successful creators post (2)_____, usually several times a week, and always try to (3)_____ with their audience through comments and messages. Choosing a specific (4)_____ helps attract the right kind of followers — people who are genuinely interested in your content. Many creators also (5)_____ with other influencers to reach new audiences. However, experts warn that focusing too much on follower (6)_____ can be discouraging. It is more important to (7)_____ a loyal, engaged community than to simply have large numbers.

grammar.explanation: First conditional: real/likely future situations. Second conditional: unreal or hypothetical situations (present/future).
rules: 1st: If + present simple, will/can/may + base verb
2nd: If + past simple, would/could/might + base verb
Use 'were' (not 'was') in formal 2nd conditional: If I were you...
1st = possible; 2nd = less likely or impossible

exercises.sentence: If you eat a balanced diet, you _____ feel much better.
If I _____ more time, I would exercise every day.
She would sleep better if she _____ her phone before bed.
If you _____ (not/warm up) before exercise, you may injure yourself.
If I _____ (be) the government, I would make sport compulsory in schools.
Unless you _____ (drink) enough water, you will become dehydrated.

reading.title: Sleep: The Forgotten Health Essential
reading.text:
Most people know that eating well and exercising regularly are essential for good health. However, one equally important factor is often overlooked: sleep. Despite its importance, surveys suggest that a significant proportion of teenagers and adults are chronically sleep-deprived.

During sleep, the body repairs itself, consolidates memories, and regulates hormones. Without enough sleep, cognitive performance deteriorates rapidly — concentration, decision-making, and even emotional control all suffer. Research has shown that people who regularly sleep fewer than seven hours per night are at greater risk of obesity, diabetes, and heart disease.

One major cause of poor sleep in modern society is the use of electronic devices before bed. The blue light emitted by screens suppresses the production of melatonin, the hormone that signals to the brain that it is time to sleep. Experts recommend avoiding screens for at least an hour before going to bed.

Developing a consistent sleep routine — going to bed and waking up at the same time every day, even at weekends — can significantly improve sleep quality. Creating a comfortable, cool, and dark sleeping environment also helps signal to the body that it is time to rest.

questions.question: What does the article suggest is often underestimated in importance?
questions.explanation: 'Sleep is often overlooked' despite being equally important to exercise and diet.

questions.question: What health risks are associated with sleeping less than 7 hours?
options: B. Obesity, diabetes, and heart disease
questions.explanation: 'Greater risk of obesity, diabetes, and heart disease.'

questions.question: How does blue light affect sleep?
options: A. It makes you feel more awake and alert during the day
B. It improves the quality of your dreams
C. It reduces the hormone that signals sleep
D. It causes you to wake up earlier
questions.explanation: 'Blue light suppresses the production of melatonin, the hormone that signals it is time to sleep.'

questions.question: What sleep strategy does the article recommend?
options: A. Sleeping as long as possible
C. Having a consistent sleep schedule
D. Taking regular naps during the day
questions.explanation: 'A consistent sleep routine can significantly improve sleep quality.'

sentence_transformations.original: Eat more vegetables and you will feel healthier.
sentence_transformations.answer: If you eat more vegetables, you will feel healthier.

sentence_transformations.original: I don't exercise regularly, so I'm not very fit.
sentence_transformations.answer: If I exercised regularly, I would be fitter.

sentence_transformations.original: She regrets not joining the gym earlier.
sentence_transformations.answer: She wishes she had joined the gym earlier.

sentence_transformations.original: Unless you rest properly, the injury will get worse.
sentence_transformations.answer: If you don't rest properly, the injury will get worse.

cloze.title: The Benefits of Regular Exercise
cloze.text: Regular exercise has numerous (1)_____ for both physical and mental health. Adults are recommended to do at least 150 minutes of (2)_____ activity per week. This doesn't have to mean going to a gym — activities such as brisk walking, swimming, or cycling all (3)_____. Exercise helps to (4)_____ the risk of many serious diseases, including heart disease and type 2 diabetes. It also improves mood by (5)_____ the release of chemicals called endorphins in the brain. For people who (6)_____ from anxiety or depression, regular exercise can be as effective as medication. The key is to (7)_____ a type of activity you enjoy so that you are more likely to keep it up.

vocabulary.definition: a general direction in which something is changing or developing
vocabulary.example: Sustainable fashion is a growing trend.

vocabulary.definition: having or showing a lot of experience and knowledge

vocabulary.definition: exactly the same

vocabulary.definition: to make someone look more attractive

vocabulary.definition: to look the same as people or things around you

vocabulary.definition: easy to recognise; clearly different from others

vocabulary.definition: clothing made in ways that don't harm the environment
vocabulary.example: More brands are adopting sustainable fashion practices.

vocabulary.definition: to make or adjust clothing to fit exactly; to customise
vocabulary.example: The suit was tailored to fit him perfectly.

vocabulary.definition: the way a surface or fabric feels when you touch it

vocabulary.example: She loves floral patterns on dresses.

grammar.explanation: Articles tell us whether we are talking about something specific (the) or unspecific (a/an), or making general statements (zero article).
rules: a/an: first mention, unspecific, jobs (She's a designer), count nouns
the: second mention, only one exists, specific thing known to both
Zero article: general statements (Fashion is important), proper nouns, most sports
a before consonant sounds; an before vowel sounds (an hour)

exercises.sentence: She is _____ fashion designer who works for _____ famous brand.
exercises.explanation: First mention, job title → 'a'; unspecific brand → 'a'.

exercises.sentence: I bought _____ pair of jeans yesterday. _____ jeans fit perfectly.
_____ fashion industry has a huge impact on _____ environment.
exercises.explanation: Specific industries → 'The'; specific environment we all share → 'the'.

exercises.sentence: She is wearing _____ amazing dress — _____ dress she bought in Milan.
exercises.explanation: 'amazing' starts with vowel sound → 'an'; second mention → 'the'.

exercises.sentence: _____ sustainable fashion is _____ answer to many environmental problems.
exercises.explanation: General concept → zero article; specific answer (unique) → 'the'.

exercises.sentence: He plays _____ tennis and reads _____ books in his spare time.
exercises.explanation: Sports and general activities → zero article.

reading.title: Fast Fashion and Its Hidden Cost
reading.text:
The term 'fast fashion' refers to the rapid production of large quantities of inexpensive clothing that is designed to be worn only a few times before being discarded. In recent decades, this model has dominated the global fashion industry, making trendy clothes accessible to a mass market.

However, the true cost of fast fashion is becoming increasingly clear. Producing cheap clothes at high speed requires enormous resources — water, energy, and raw materials. The fashion industry is estimated to be responsible for around 10% of global carbon emissions, more than the aviation and shipping industries combined.

The social impact is equally troubling. Many garments are produced in factories where workers face poor conditions and extremely low wages. Consumers in wealthy countries enjoy cheap prices, but workers in developing nations often pay a high human price.

In response, a growing movement of conscious consumers is choosing to shop secondhand, buy from ethical brands, or simply buy less. Some fashion designers are exploring innovative materials, such as fabric made from recycled plastic bottles, to make the industry more sustainable. Whether these efforts will be enough to transform the fashion industry remains to be seen.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of fast fashion?
options: A. High-quality clothing that lasts a long time
B. Cheap clothing designed to be worn briefly
C. Clothing made from sustainable materials
D. Clothing designed by famous fashion houses
questions.explanation: 'Large quantities of inexpensive clothing designed to be worn only a few times.'

questions.question: What does the article say about the fashion industry's carbon emissions?
options: A. They are less than aviation emissions
B. They are equal to shipping emissions
C. They exceed aviation and shipping combined
D. They have been falling in recent years
questions.explanation: 'More than the aviation and shipping industries combined.'

questions.question: How does the article describe factory workers in developing countries?
options: B. They have good working conditions
C. They work in poor conditions for low wages
D. They benefit from fast fashion's popularity
questions.explanation: 'Workers face poor conditions and extremely low wages.'

questions.question: What is one way people are responding to fast fashion's problems?
options: C. Shopping secondhand or buying from ethical brands
questions.explanation: 'Conscious consumers choosing to shop secondhand, buy from ethical brands, or buy less.'

sentence_transformations.original: Buying secondhand clothes is not as expensive as buying new ones.
sentence_transformations.answer: Buying secondhand clothes is cheaper than buying new ones.

sentence_transformations.original: It was the most elegant dress I had ever seen.
sentence_transformations.answer: I had never seen such an elegant dress.

sentence_transformations.original: She couldn't decide which dress to wear.
sentence_transformations.answer: She had difficulty deciding which dress to wear.

sentence_transformations.original: The jacket needs to be dry-cleaned.
sentence_transformations.answer: She needs to have the jacket dry-cleaned.

cloze.title: Dressing for Success
cloze.text: Research suggests that what we wear can affect not only how others (1)_____ us, but also how we feel about ourselves. This phenomenon, sometimes called 'enclothed cognition', suggests that clothing has a psychological (2)_____ on the wearer. For example, wearing smart, professional clothing can make people feel more (3)_____ and authoritative in work situations. On the other hand, wearing comfortable, casual clothing at home can help us (4)_____. Most experts agree that dressing in a way that reflects your personality is the most important thing. Trying to (5)_____ a certain image that doesn't suit you can actually (6)_____ your confidence. The key is to wear clothes that make you feel comfortable and (7)_____ in your own skin.

vocabulary.definition: the variety of plant and animal life in a particular habitat
vocabulary.example: The Amazon rainforest has incredible biodiversity.

vocabulary.definition: all the living organisms in an area and how they interact
vocabulary.example: Coral reefs are one of the world's most important ecosystems.

vocabulary.definition: cutting down large areas of forest
vocabulary.example: Deforestation is destroying wildlife habitats.

vocabulary.definition: replaced naturally and never running out
vocabulary.example: Solar and wind are forms of renewable energy.

vocabulary.definition: the natural environment of a plant or animal
vocabulary.example: Polar bears are losing their natural habitat.

vocabulary.definition: a group of plants or animals that are the same type
vocabulary.example: Thousands of species are threatened with extinction.

vocabulary.definition: the amount of carbon dioxide produced by a person or organisation
vocabulary.example: Switching to public transport reduces your carbon footprint.

vocabulary.definition: no longer existing as a species
vocabulary.example: Dinosaurs became extinct 66 million years ago.

vocabulary.definition: the protection of natural environments and species
vocabulary.example: Conservation efforts have helped tiger populations recover.

vocabulary.definition: a long period of time with little or no rain
vocabulary.example: The drought destroyed the harvest in the region.

vocabulary.definition: harmful substances in the environment
vocabulary.example: Air pollution is a major health problem in cities.

vocabulary.definition: not harmful to the environment in the long term
vocabulary.example: We need more sustainable energy sources.

vocabulary.definition: the warming of the Earth due to trapped heat from carbon dioxide
vocabulary.example: Human activity is intensifying the greenhouse effect.

vocabulary.definition: illegally hunting or catching animals
vocabulary.example: Poaching is threatening the survival of elephants.

vocabulary.definition: a slow-moving mass of ice
vocabulary.example: Glaciers are melting faster than expected.

grammar.topic: Future Forms (will, going to, Present Continuous, Future Perfect)
grammar.explanation: English uses several structures to talk about the future, each with a different meaning.
rules: will: instant decisions, predictions, promises, offers
going to: plans already decided, predictions based on evidence
Present Continuous: fixed arrangements in the near future
Future Perfect: will + have + past participle (completed by a time)

exercises.sentence: Look at those clouds! It _____ (rain) soon.
I promise I _____ recycle more from now on.
We _____ (fly) to Brazil next Monday for the conference.
exercises.explanation: Fixed arrangement with date → Present Continuous.

exercises.sentence: By 2050, many coastal cities _____ (flood) if emissions don't fall.
exercises.explanation: Completed by a future time → Future Perfect.

exercises.sentence: 'The heating has stopped.' 'OK, I _____ call the engineer.'
exercises.explanation: Instant decision made at the moment of speaking → 'will'.

exercises.sentence: Scientists predict that average temperatures _____ rise by 2°C this century.

reading.title: Rewilding: Nature's Comeback
reading.text:
In recent decades, a radical new approach to conservation has gained momentum: rewilding. Unlike traditional conservation, which focuses on protecting existing habitats, rewilding involves actively restoring natural ecosystems by reintroducing species that have disappeared and allowing nature to manage itself.

One famous example is the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park in the USA in 1995. Before the wolves' return, deer populations had grown unchecked, overgrazing vegetation along riverbanks. When the wolves returned, they changed the behaviour of the deer, which began avoiding certain areas. This allowed plants and trees to regenerate, which in turn stabilised riverbanks, improved water quality, and increased populations of birds and fish. Scientists call this a 'trophic cascade' — a chain reaction triggered by a top predator.

In Europe, rewilding projects are restoring forests, wetlands, and grasslands across the continent. In Scotland, discussions are underway about reintroducing lynx, which disappeared around 1,300 years ago. Supporters argue that this would help control deer populations and restore natural balance.

Critics, however, raise concerns about the risks of reintroducing large predators, particularly in areas where people and livestock live nearby. The debate continues, but evidence from successful projects suggests that rewilding may be one of the most powerful tools we have to reverse biodiversity loss.

questions.question: What is rewilding different from traditional conservation?
options: B. It only works in national parks
C. It actively restores ecosystems with species reintroduction
D. It focuses on protecting existing habitats
questions.explanation: 'Rewilding involves actively restoring ecosystems by reintroducing species.'

questions.question: How did the reintroduction of wolves benefit Yellowstone?
options: A. It reduced the wolf population
B. It had no measurable effect
C. It improved water quality and biodiversity
D. It eliminated deer from the park
questions.explanation: 'Allowed plants to regenerate, stabilised riverbanks, improved water quality, and increased bird and fish populations.'

questions.question: What concerns do critics have about rewilding?
options: B. Predators might threaten people and animals nearby
C. There are no suitable habitats
questions.explanation: 'Risks of reintroducing large predators in areas where people and livestock live nearby.'

questions.question: What does the article suggest about rewilding overall?
options: A. It is too controversial to implement
B. It is only suitable for wilderness areas
C. It could be a powerful tool against biodiversity loss
D. It has failed in most cases
questions.explanation: 'Rewilding may be one of the most powerful tools to reverse biodiversity loss.'

sentence_transformations.original: Scientists expect that the glacier will disappear within 20 years.
sentence_transformations.answer: The glacier is predicted to disappear within 20 years.

sentence_transformations.original: Unless we act now, we will lose many more species.
sentence_transformations.answer: If we don't act now, we will lose many more species.

sentence_transformations.original: The government plans to reduce emissions by 50% by 2030.
sentence_transformations.answer: Emissions will be reduced by 50% by 2030.

sentence_transformations.original: It is the most biodiverse region on the planet.
sentence_transformations.answer: Nowhere on the planet is as biodiverse as this region.

cloze.title: Protecting Our Oceans
cloze.text: Oceans (1)_____ more than 70% of the Earth's surface and are home to an enormous (2)_____ of species. However, they are (3)_____ serious threats including plastic pollution, overfishing, and climate change. Plastic waste in particular is a growing (4)_____ — millions of tonnes of plastic (5)_____ the oceans each year, harming marine life and entering the food chain. Many countries are now taking (6)_____ to reduce single-use plastic, such as banning plastic bags and straws. Protecting our oceans is not just an environmental (7)_____ — it is essential for the survival of future generations.

vocabulary.definition: able to express ideas clearly; to express clearly
vocabulary.example: She is very articulate when presenting her ideas.

vocabulary.definition: to convince someone to do or believe something
vocabulary.example: He managed to persuade the committee to support his plan.

vocabulary.definition: to give reasons to support or oppose an idea; to disagree
vocabulary.example: She argued that the decision was unfair.

vocabulary.definition: to give more details about something
vocabulary.example: Could you elaborate on your point?

vocabulary.definition: to communicate a message or feeling
vocabulary.example: It's important to convey your ideas clearly.

vocabulary.definition: able to speak several languages
vocabulary.example: Being multilingual is a valuable skill in business.

vocabulary.definition: a form of a language spoken in a particular area
vocabulary.example: The novel is written in a regional dialect.

vocabulary.definition: a subtle difference in meaning or expression
vocabulary.example: Poetry captures nuances that plain prose cannot.

vocabulary.definition: the quality of a voice or piece of writing that shows attitude
vocabulary.example: The formal tone of her email was impressive.

vocabulary.definition: to translate spoken language; to understand the meaning of something
vocabulary.example: She works as an interpreter at the United Nations.

vocabulary.definition: able to speak two languages fluently
vocabulary.example: Growing up, he was bilingual in French and Arabic.

vocabulary.definition: logical and well-organised; easy to understand
vocabulary.example: Please make sure your argument is coherent.

vocabulary.definition: the art of using language persuasively
vocabulary.example: Politicians often use rhetoric to persuade audiences.

vocabulary.definition: expressing ideas in a very clear and effective way
vocabulary.example: The speaker gave an eloquent defence of her position.

vocabulary.definition: to suggest something without saying it directly
vocabulary.example: He didn't say I was wrong, but he implied it.

grammar.explanation: Mixed conditionals combine different time frames. Wish/If only express regret or desire about past or present situations.
rules: Mixed: If I had studied harder (past), I would have a better job now (present).
Wish + past simple: wish for a different present (I wish I were taller.)
Wish + past perfect: regret about the past (I wish I hadn't said that.)
Wish + would: desire for change in someone's behaviour (I wish he would listen.)
If only = stronger form of wish

exercises.sentence: I wish I _____ (study) more languages when I was young.
exercises.explanation: Regret about past → wish + past perfect.

exercises.sentence: If she _____ (not/give up) early, she would be fluent by now.
exercises.explanation: Mixed conditional: past action affecting present result.

exercises.sentence: If only I _____ (be) more confident when speaking in public!
exercises.explanation: Wish about present state → if only + past simple (were).

exercises.sentence: I wish my teacher _____ (explain) things more slowly.
exercises.explanation: Desire for behavioural change → wish + would.

exercises.sentence: If he _____ (tell) me earlier, I could have helped him.
exercises.explanation: Past unreal → 3rd conditional: If + past perfect.

exercises.sentence: She _____ the meeting if she _____ the invitation. (miss / receive)
exercises.explanation: 3rd conditional: would have + past participle / if + past perfect.

reading.title: Why Learn a Foreign Language?
reading.text:
In an age of increasingly sophisticated translation technology, some people question whether learning a foreign language is still worthwhile. After all, if a smartphone can translate any text or conversation instantly, why spend years studying grammar and vocabulary?

However, linguists and educators argue that translation technology misses much of what makes language valuable. Language is not simply a code for exchanging information — it is the vehicle through which culture, humour, and emotion are expressed. A machine can translate words, but it struggles to capture the nuance, irony, and cultural context that make communication truly meaningful.

Furthermore, research consistently shows significant cognitive benefits from learning foreign languages. Bilingual individuals tend to be better at problem-solving, multi-tasking, and switching between different tasks. Studies also suggest that speaking more than one language may delay the onset of dementia by several years.

From a practical standpoint, knowing a second language opens doors professionally. In a globalised world, employers in many sectors actively seek candidates who can communicate across linguistic and cultural boundaries. For students considering future careers in business, diplomacy, science, or the arts, foreign language skills are an undeniable asset.

The digital age has made language learning more accessible than ever — apps, online courses, and language exchange partners are available to anyone with an internet connection. The question is no longer whether we can learn a language, but whether we choose to.

questions.question: What argument do some people make against language learning?
options: A. It is too difficult for most people
B. Translation technology can do it instead
C. There are not enough language teachers
D. English is spoken everywhere now
questions.explanation: 'If a smartphone can translate any text, why spend years studying?'

questions.question: What do linguists say technology cannot capture?
options: C. Cultural nuance and emotional meaning
questions.explanation: 'Machines struggle to capture the nuance, irony, and cultural context.'

questions.question: What brain benefit does the article mention for bilinguals?
questions.explanation: 'Bilingual individuals tend to be better at problem-solving and multi-tasking.'

questions.question: What is the article's conclusion about language learning today?
options: A. It has become too difficult
B. Technology has made it unnecessary
C. It is more accessible than ever
D. Only young children can learn a new language
questions.explanation: 'The digital age has made language learning more accessible than ever.'

sentence_transformations.original: I regret not taking Spanish classes when I had the chance.
sentence_transformations.answer: I wish I had taken Spanish classes when I had the chance.

sentence_transformations.original: Despite speaking three languages, she found the translation difficult.
sentence_transformations.answer: Although she speaks three languages, she found the translation difficult.

sentence_transformations.original: It is said that bilingual people have better memories.
sentence_transformations.answer: It is believed that bilingual people have better memories.

sentence_transformations.original: He was the most eloquent speaker at the conference.
sentence_transformations.answer: No one at the conference had spoken more eloquently than him.
sentence_transformations.hint: No one had spoken more eloquently

cloze.title: The Art of Persuasion
cloze.text: Being able to (1)_____ others effectively is one of the most valuable skills you can have, both in everyday life and in a professional (2)_____. Whether you are trying to convince a friend, write a compelling essay, or present an idea to your boss, the principles of persuasion are largely the same. Firstly, it is important to clearly (3)_____ your argument with relevant evidence and examples. Secondly, acknowledging the other person's point of (4)_____ shows that you have considered their perspective. Finally, choosing the right (5)_____ is crucial — being too aggressive or too passive will both undermine your message. The most (6)_____ communicators are those who combine logic with empathy, presenting facts while also (7)_____ to the emotions of their audience.
